source: Utah State Construction Code, IRC/IBC as adopted, local jurisdiction amendments — HB65 2024 session updates

state_code.base_code: 2021 International Residential Code (IRC) with Utah amendments; 2024 IBC/IRC adopted via HB65 effective 2026
state_code.fire_code: 2021 International Fire Code (IFC) with Utah amendments
state_code.mechanical_code: 2021 International Mechanical Code (IMC) with Utah amendments
key_utah_amendments: R301.2.3 Snow loads: Where ground snow loads exceed 75 psf, eaves must sustain 2x psf on overhangs
R301.6 Utah Snow Loads: Ground snow loads per local jurisdiction or ASCE 7 Figure 7.2-1
R313.1 Sprinkler systems: Not required for one- and two-family dwellings unless local jurisdiction requires
R602.10.1 Braced wall panels: Continuous structural panel sheathing method allowed as alternative
R802.10.4 Truss design: Trusses must be designed by registered engineer, sealed drawings required
Energy code: Prescriptive R-values for Climate Zone 5 (most of Utah) and Zone 6 (mountain areas)
HB65 2024: Adopts 2024 IBC, 2024 IECC, 2024 IRC — phased rollout, check jurisdiction for effective date
Utah snow load formula: Pf = 0.7 x Ce x Ct x I x Pg (ASCE 7); flat roof snow load = 0.7 x ground snow load minimum

zone_5.window_notes: Triple-pane windows strongly recommended for custom homes in Zone 5 mountain-adjacent areas

zone_6.ceiling_insulation: R-49 minimum — R-60 recommended for high-altitude custom homes
zone_6.window_notes: Triple-pane windows with U-0.22 or better strongly recommended; heated window frames in extreme snow areas

summit_county.name: Summit County
summit_county.permit_fees_notes: Fees based on valuation — typically $8,000-$25,000+ for large custom homes
special_requirements: Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI) requirements apply in most mountain areas — Class A roofing required
Steep slope (>15%) building requires geotechnical report from licensed engineer
Snow country design: roof snow loads often exceed 100 psf, drift loads must be calculated per ASCE 7
Engineered foundation required for most custom homes — frost depth 36 inches minimum
Radon mitigation system required in new construction — passive system minimum, active recommended
Fire sprinklers required if fire district response time exceeds 5 minutes or access road exceeds 1,000 ft
Defensible space requirements in WUI zones — 30 ft zone 1, 100 ft zone 2
Driveway grade maximum 12% for fire apparatus access
Turnaround required for driveways over 150 ft
Septic system: OWTS permit from Summit County Health — perc test required
Well permit from Utah Division of Water Rights if not on municipal water
Elevation certificate required for flood zone properties
Solar-ready conduit required for new construction
EV charging rough-in required for new garages
permit_requirements: Building permit application with 2 sets of stamped engineered plans
Soils/geotechnical report for hillside construction or lots with slopes >15%
SWPPP (Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan) for sites > 1 acre — required before grading
Septic permit from Summit County Health Department
Access permit from county road department if new driveway
Grading permit for significant earthwork
Zoning compliance letter from planning department
Structural calculations stamped by Utah-licensed PE
Energy compliance report (REScheck or equivalent)
custom_home_notes: Most custom homes in Summit County require full structural engineering — not prescriptive IRC
Snow drift calculations critical — valleys between roof sections accumulate 2-3x ground snow load
Steel moment frames common for large open floor plans due to seismic requirements
Radiant floor heat is standard in high-end custom homes — plan for slab insulation
Heated driveways and walkways common — plan electrical or hydronic system early
Dark sky ordinance in some areas — exterior lighting must be downward-facing
Architectural review required in many HOA areas (Promontory, Glenwild, Tuhaye, etc.)

park_city.name: Park City
special_requirements: Historic district overlay (Old Town) — design review required, strict architectural guidelines
Height restrictions: 28 ft in most residential zones, 35 ft in some mountain zones — measured from grade
Snow storage requirements: 10% of lot area must be designated for snow storage
Heated driveway/walkway requirements in some steep zones — check with planning
Green building incentives: LEED or equivalent may qualify for expedited review
Dark sky ordinance — all exterior lighting must be full cutoff fixtures
Wildland-Urban Interface (WUI) code applies to most of Park City
Class A fire-rated roofing required in WUI zones
Defensible space: 30 ft zone 1 clearance required
Steep slope overlay: lots >30% slope require additional geotechnical review
View corridor protection in some zones — check with planning before design
Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs) allowed in most zones — separate permit required
permit_requirements: Online permit application through Park City permit portal
2 sets of stamped engineered plans for custom homes
Energy compliance documentation — REScheck or COMcheck
Soils report for hillside lots
SWPPP for sites over 1 acre
Architectural review application if in design review overlay
Historic district application if in Old Town
Structural calculations by Utah-licensed PE
custom_home_notes: Park City is one of the most regulated custom home markets in Utah — budget extra time for approvals
Architectural review in many neighborhoods adds 4-8 weeks to approval timeline
Snow loads: 100 psf ground, but drift loads at roof valleys can reach 150-200 psf — engineer required
Luxury custom homes common — high-end finishes, smart home systems, wine cellars, home theaters
Ski-in/ski-out lots near Deer Valley and PCMR have additional easement requirements
Radiant heat standard — both floor and driveway in high-end builds
Steel structural systems common for large spans and open floor plans
Geothermal systems popular for heating/cooling in Park City custom homes

deer_valley.name: Deer Valley / Deer Crest / Empire Pass
deer_valley.building_department: Summit County Community Development (unincorporated) or Park City Building Dept (within city limits)
deer_valley.note: Deer Valley straddles Summit County and Park City boundaries — verify jurisdiction before permitting
deer_valley.plan_review_time_weeks: 6-12 weeks — complex projects may take longer
special_requirements: Verify jurisdiction first: lower Deer Valley (Deer Valley Dr area) = Park City; upper areas (Empire Pass, Deer Crest) = Summit County
Deer Crest HOA has strict architectural review — Deer Crest Architectural Control Committee approval required
Empire Pass: Talisker Land Holdings has separate CC&Rs and architectural review process
Deer Valley East Village (Mayflower): Summit County jurisdiction — new development with specific design guidelines
Extreme snow loads at elevation — 115 psf ground, drift loads at roof valleys can exceed 200 psf
All structural systems must be fully engineered — no prescriptive IRC allowed at this elevation
Ski easements and ski run setbacks apply to many lots — check title report
Avalanche hazard zones exist in Empire Pass and upper Deer Crest — geotech and avalanche study may be required
WUI requirements: Class A roofing, ignition-resistant construction, defensible space mandatory
Fire sprinkler systems required in most Deer Valley custom homes due to access limitations
Heated driveways and walkways essentially mandatory at this elevation for safety
Snowmelt systems for decks and entries standard in luxury builds
Dark sky ordinance — full cutoff exterior lighting required
Minimum lot size varies by zone — check with county/city planning
permit_requirements: Full structural engineering package — stamped by Utah-licensed PE
Geotechnical/soils report — required for all lots
Avalanche hazard assessment if in mapped hazard zone
Architectural review approval from HOA/CC&R authority before permit submission
SWPPP for sites over 1 acre
Septic permit if not on sewer (many upper Deer Valley lots are on septic)
Energy compliance documentation
Grading and drainage plan
Access/driveway plan showing grade and turnaround
custom_home_notes: Deer Valley is Utah's most prestigious custom home market — projects typically $3M-$20M+
Steel moment frames and heavy timber are common structural systems for large spans
Snow management is critical: heated roofs, gutters, driveways, walkways, decks all need snowmelt
Radiant floor heat is standard — hydronic systems preferred over electric
Ski room/boot dryers, equipment storage, and ski-in/ski-out access are common program elements
Mechanical rooms must be sized for complex systems — radiant, snowmelt, generator, AV, smart home
Generator backup power is standard — mountain power outages common in winter storms
Water features (indoor/outdoor pools, hot tubs) common — plan for structural loads and mechanical space
Deer Valley East Village (Mayflower) is a new development with modern design guidelines — contact Summit County for current requirements
Construction season is limited: May-November for exterior work — plan schedule carefully
Material delivery to upper elevations can be challenging — plan for crane access and staging areas

powder_mountain.name: Powder Mountain / Eden / Ogden Valley
special_requirements: Extreme snow loads — 150 psf ground at base, 200+ psf at summit elevations
Roof design MUST account for unbalanced snow loads and drift — ASCE 7 Chapter 7 required
Metal roofing with snow guards strongly recommended — standing seam metal standard
Structural engineer required for ALL custom homes — no prescriptive IRC at these loads
Access road grades limited to 12% maximum for emergency vehicle access
Heated driveways required or strongly recommended for grades over 6%
Fire district: Ogden Valley Fire — sprinklers required if response time exceeds 5 min
Avalanche hazard zones exist on steep slopes — check USGS maps before design
Well and septic common — percolation test and OWTS permit required
Weber County has strict grading ordinance — limit disturbance to minimize erosion
Dark sky requirements in Ogden Valley — preserve night sky quality
Powder Mountain resort has its own CC&Rs for lots within the resort boundary
permit_requirements: Weber County building permit application
Stamped structural engineering plans
Soils/geotechnical report
SWPPP for sites over 1 acre
OWTS (septic) permit from Weber-Morgan Health Department
Well permit from Utah Division of Water Rights
Grading permit for significant earthwork
Access permit if new driveway on county road
custom_home_notes: Powder Mountain is becoming a premier ski resort community — custom home market growing rapidly
150 psf ground snow load means roof structural systems must be heavily engineered
Typical roof dead load + 150 psf snow = 170-180 psf total — steel or heavy timber often required
Snowmelt systems for driveways and entries are essentially mandatory at upper elevations
Construction season: May-October — winter construction very difficult at elevation
Powder Mountain resort village has specific architectural guidelines — contact resort for CC&Rs

morgan_county.name: Morgan County / Morgan City
special_requirements: IRC adopted with Utah state amendments — one of the more straightforward jurisdictions
One- and two-family dwellings per IRC Chapter 11 (energy) — Zone 5 requirements
Automatic fire sprinklers per IRC R313 as amended by Utah state — check current requirement
Soils report may be required for expansive soils (common in Morgan Valley)
Flood zone check required — FEMA maps, Morgan River corridor has flood zones
Agricultural exemptions may apply for outbuildings on agricultural-zoned parcels
Septic systems common — OWTS permit from Weber-Morgan Health Department
Well permits from Utah Division of Water Rights for rural lots
Hillside lots (east and west canyon walls) require geotechnical report
Wildfire risk in canyon areas — WUI requirements may apply
permit_requirements: Building permit application — can be submitted in person or online
2 sets of plans — engineered plans required for custom homes
Energy compliance documentation
Soils report if required by building official
SWPPP for sites over 1 acre
OWTS permit if on septic
Well permit if applicable
custom_home_notes: Morgan County is more affordable than Park City/Summit County — growing custom home market
60 psf ground snow load is manageable — engineered trusses handle it well
Morgan Valley has beautiful views and rural character — popular for acreage custom homes
East Canyon and Lost Creek areas have higher snow loads — verify with county
Commute to Salt Lake City is 30-45 minutes — popular for families wanting rural lifestyle
Agricultural land conversion to residential may require zone change — plan ahead
Water rights are critical in Morgan County — verify water availability before purchase

layton.name: Layton City
special_requirements: Standard IRC/IBC with Utah state amendments
Residential plan review typically 2-3 weeks
Impact fees apply for new construction — verify current schedule
Landscaping requirements for new homes — 50% of front yard must be landscaped
Driveway approach permit from public works required
Hillside development ordinance for lots on Wasatch Front benches
permit_requirements: Online permit application through Layton City portal
2 sets of plans
Energy compliance documentation
Soils report for hillside lots

salt_lake_city.name: Salt Lake City
special_requirements: Historic preservation overlay in many neighborhoods — design review required
ADU (Accessory Dwelling Unit) regulations — allowed in most zones, separate permit
Solar-ready requirements for new construction
EV charging infrastructure requirements for new garages
Stormwater management requirements — detention/retention may be required
Tree protection ordinance during construction — permit for removal of significant trees
permit_requirements: Online permit application
2 sets of plans
Energy compliance documentation
Historic review if in overlay district

coalville.name: Coalville City
coalville.building_department: Coalville City / Summit County Community Development
special_requirements: Small city — uses Summit County building department for inspections
Zoning check required before permit application
Water and sewer connection fees — verify capacity
Moderate snow loads — engineered trusses recommended
Proximity to I-80 corridor — some lots have noise considerations
permit_requirements: Submit to Summit County Community Development
2 sets of plans
Energy compliance documentation

wall_framing.studs: 2x4 or 2x6 at 16 in OC (24 in OC allowed for some conditions); 2x6 required for Zone 6 energy compliance
wall_framing.top_plates: Double top plate required, single allowed with approved connectors
wall_framing.bottom_plate: Pressure-treated on concrete, standard on wood subfloor

headers.over_10ft: Engineered header required — LVL, steel, or glulam

wall_framing.bracing: Per IRC R602.10 — continuous sheathing or let-in bracing; engineered shear walls for high seismic/wind
wall_framing.fire_blocking: Required at 10ft vertical intervals, at floor/ceiling levels, at soffits, and at connections between horizontal and vertical spaces

floor_framing.joists: Per IRC span tables — species, grade, spacing, and load determine size
floor_framing.bridging: Required at 8ft intervals for joists deeper than 2x12
floor_framing.subfloor: 3/4 in T&G plywood or OSB, glued and nailed
floor_framing.cantilever: Maximum 1/4 of backspan for floor joists

roof_framing.rafters: Per IRC span tables or engineered
roof_framing.collar_ties: Required in upper 1/3 of attic space, 4ft OC maximum
roof_framing.ridge_board: Minimum 1x, depth equal to cut end of rafter
roof_framing.ridge_beam: Required for cathedral ceilings without collar ties — must be engineered
roof_framing.sheathing: 7/16 in OSB or 1/2 in plywood minimum, H-clips at unsupported edges
roof_framing.high_snow_load_notes: For loads >75 psf: engineered trusses or rafters required; drift loads at valleys and parapets must be calculated; consider heated roof systems to prevent ice dams

fastener_schedule.sheathing_to_framing_walls: 8d common at 6 in OC edges, 12 in OC field
fastener_schedule.sheathing_to_framing_roof: 8d common at 6 in OC edges, 12 in OC field
fastener_schedule.subfloor_to_joist: 8d common at 6 in OC edges, 12 in OC field (plus construction adhesive)

steel_erection_utah.general: Steel erection in Utah follows AISC 303 Code of Standard Practice and OSHA 1926 Subpart R
high_altitude_considerations: Snow loads at Deer Valley, Powder Mountain, and Summit County require heavier steel sections than Wasatch Front
Wind exposure category C or D at mountain elevations — check ASCE 7 wind maps
Seismic Design Category D throughout most of Utah — moment connections required for lateral resistance
Thermal expansion/contraction significant at mountain elevations — expansion joints required for long buildings
Crane access can be challenging at mountain sites — plan for terrain and road grade limits

common_beam_applications.residential_floor_beams: W8, W10, W12 most common for residential spans 10-20 ft
common_beam_applications.residential_ridge_beams: W10, W12, W14 for cathedral ceiling ridge beams
common_beam_applications.commercial_floor_framing: W14, W16, W18 for spans 20-40 ft
common_beam_applications.moment_frames: W14 columns with W18-W24 beams typical for seismic moment frames
common_beam_applications.long_span_beams: W24, W27, W30 for spans 40-60 ft; W33, W36 for 60+ ft spans
common_beam_applications.custom_home_steel: W12x26 to W12x50 common for residential ridge and floor beams; W14x48 to W14x82 for moment frame columns

connection_requirements: All moment connections require engineer design and inspection
High-strength bolts: A325 or A490 per AISC/RCSC specification
Welds: E70XX electrodes minimum; weld inspection required for seismic connections
Base plates: anchor bolt pattern per engineer design; grout under base plate required
Shear tabs and clip angles for simple shear connections
Pre-qualified connections per AISC 358 for seismic moment frames

custom_home_cost_benchmarks_utah.note: Approximate ranges as of 2025-2026 — verify with current supplier quotes

lumber_prices_approximate.note: Prices fluctuate — always get current quotes

inspection_sequence: 1. Footing inspection — before concrete pour, verify rebar, depth, width
2. Foundation inspection — forms, rebar, anchor bolts before pour
4. Slab inspection — vapor barrier, rebar, thickness
5. Framing inspection — ALL framing complete, sheathing, nailing, connectors, fire blocking
6. Steel inspection — if steel structure, special inspection required for welds and bolts
7. Rough plumbing — supply and DWV before cover
8. Rough electrical — wiring, boxes, panels before cover
9. Rough mechanical — ductwork, equipment before cover
10. Insulation inspection — R-values, vapor barrier, air sealing
12. Final inspections — plumbing, electrical, mechanical, building
common_code_violations_framing: Missing or incorrect hurricane ties/straps at roof-to-wall connection
Missing fire blocking at floor levels and soffits
Incorrect nailing schedule — too few nails or wrong size
Missing holdowns at shear wall ends
Studs not aligned with joists/trusses above (stacking)
Missing cripple studs below window sills
Notching or boring joists/studs beyond code limits
Incorrect Simpson connector installation — missing nails, wrong nail type
Snow drift loads not calculated at roof valleys and parapets (critical in Utah mountain areas)
Insufficient bearing length for beams at supports
Missing shear transfer hardware at floor-to-wall connections in high seismic zones
